tabs.name: Overview
tabs.intro: How food becomes you — the single mental model under everything else.

blocks.x: Strip nutrition down to first principles and it is one loop: you eat food, your body dismantles it for energy and for raw materials, and it uses both to run and rebuild itself. Everything in this library — macros, micros, cholesterol, stress, metabolism — hangs off that loop.

blocks.cap: Food is taken apart for fuel and building blocks.

blocks.x: The one equation: food → ATP

Every single thing your body does — a heartbeat, a thought, a muscle twitch, an immune cell fighting infection — is paid for in ATP. Food is just stored chemical energy; ATP is the spendable cash your cells actually use.

blocks.cap: Three layers: raw food → the conversion → the spendable currency.

args.caption: Glucose is the ore; ATP is the refined, spendable metal.

blocks.x: Calories = the total energy locked in food. ATP = the portion your cells actually capture. Heat = the rest, lost in conversion — and what keeps you at 37°C.
Two directions at once
Metabolism is two opposite processes running simultaneously — one tearing down for energy, one building up for repair.

items.x: Break down → release energy. Digesting food, burning fat, breaking down muscle.
Build up → consume energy. Building muscle, repairing tissue, making hormones.
Catabolism + anabolism, happening together, every second you are alive.

blocks.x: The fuel hierarchy
Your body is a hybrid engine — it runs on a mix of fuels, but in a preference order.

steps: Fat — largest store, slower to access (9 kcal/g vs 4)
Protein — last resort, pulled from muscle when desperate

blocks.x: How this library is organised

items.x: Calories, ATP, BMR/TDEE and what actually moves your metabolic rate.
Carbs, protein, fats and sugar — the fuel and the framework.
Vitamins, minerals and the way they help or block each other.
Cholesterol and the stress response — where it all connects.
The common conditions, decoded from first principles.

tabs.name: Energy & Metabolism
tabs.intro: Where your energy comes from, and what sets how much you burn.

topics.name: Calories → ATP
topics.blurb: How food energy becomes usable energy.

blocks.x: A calorie is just a unit of energy. The number on a food label is the total chemical energy stored in the bonds of its glucose, fat and protein. But your body can't spend calories directly — it has to convert them, inside your cells, into ATP.

blocks.cap: Cellular respiration — the core energy reaction.

blocks.x: Carbs, fats and proteins all funnel into this same ATP-producing machinery, just at different entry points. Fat yields more ATP per gram (that's why it's 9 kcal/g vs 4 for carbs and protein), but glucose is the quickest to tap.

steps: Fats → fatty acids → ATP (more per gram, slower)
Proteins → amino acids → ATP only when needed

topics.name: Metabolism & TDEE
topics.blurb: The four things you burn calories on.

blocks.x: Your Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE) is everything you burn in a day. Most people picture 'exercise' — but exercise is usually the smallest, most variable slice. Four components make up the whole.

head: Component
Share of TDEE
What it is
rows: Energy just to stay alive at rest — heart, lungs, kidneys, cells.
Walking around, fidgeting, standing, gesturing. The quiet giant.
The cost of digesting and processing food. Yes, eating burns calories.

blocks.x: The two biggest levers — BMR and NEAT — are the ones people ignore. An hour in the gym can be undone by sitting still for the other 23.

topics.name: Move Your Metabolic Rate
topics.blurb: What raises it, what lowers it.

blocks.x: Metabolic rate isn't fixed. Modern life — sedentary, sleep-deprived, stressed, low-protein — systematically pushes it down. Almost everyone's goal is to nudge it back up.

blocks.cap: The levers that raise metabolic rate.

items.x: More muscle · resistance training · high protein · adequate thyroid (T3/T4) · good sleep · cold exposure · youth · caffeine · post-exercise EPOC.
Low muscle (sarcopenia) · chronic caloric restriction · hypothyroidism · chronic stress (cortisol) · poor sleep · aging · sedentary living.

blocks.x: Muscle mass is the single most powerful lever you control. It's the 'idling speed' of the whole engine.

topics.name: The Muscle–Metabolism Loop
topics.blurb: Why muscle is the master lever.

blocks.x: Muscle isn't just for looks or strength — it's metabolically active tissue that burns calories at rest and improves how you handle glucose. It sets off a self-reinforcing cycle in whichever direction you're heading.

blocks.cap: The upward spiral — and it runs in reverse too.

blocks.x: Concretely: two people at 70 kg, one at 30% body fat and one at 15%, differ by ~10 kg of muscle — and the leaner one burns roughly 300 more calories a day just existing. Over a year that's ~14 kg of fat's worth of energy, for doing nothing extra. This is why resistance training is the strongest long-term metabolic intervention there is.
The three hormonal accelerators

items.x: Adequate T3 = every cell runs at full speed. Low T3 (hypothyroidism) can drop metabolic rate 30–40% — fatigue, cold, weight gain, brain fog. Needs iodine + selenium.
Chronic cortisol breaks down muscle, stores visceral fat, suppresses thyroid and wrecks sleep — lowering BMR from several angles at once.
One bad night drops insulin sensitivity; several drop growth hormone, raise cortisol and cut muscle repair — measurably lowering metabolic rate.

blocks.x: The reverse — the sarcopenia death spiral: less muscle → lower burn → more fat → worse insulin sensitivity → higher cortisol → even less muscle. The direction you feed wins.

tabs.name: Macronutrients
tabs.intro: The three fuels and the framework — carbs, protein, fats, and sugar.

blocks.x: Carbohydrates are the body's quickest fuel. Nearly all of them end up as glucose in your blood — 'blood sugar' is literally the concentration of glucose, measured in mg/dL (a healthy fasting value is around 95).
When blood glucose rises, the pancreas releases insulin — the master storage hormone. It does three jobs at once, deciding where the glucose goes.

blocks.cap: Eat carbs → glucose rises → insulin stores it three ways.

blocks.x: The blood-sugar response tells the story
A longer-term marker is HbA1c — the fraction of your haemoglobin that has glucose stuck to it. Because red cells live ~3 months, it's a running average of your blood sugar, not a single snapshot.
The problem is rarely carbs existing — it's refined carbs and sugar spiking glucose fast and often, forcing big insulin swings.

topics.name: Protein
topics.blurb: Amino acids — the body's building material.

blocks.x: Protein is chains of amino acids — the alphabet the body builds nearly everything from: muscle, enzymes, hormones, skin, hair, antibodies, even neurotransmitters. There are 20 amino acids; 9 are essential (you must eat them) and 11 are non-essential (the body makes them).

blocks.cap: 20 amino acids — 9 you must eat, 11 you can make.

blocks.x: Complete vs incomplete

items.x: Eggs (gold standard), whey, milk & paneer, chicken, fish.
Dal/lentils, rice/roti, peanut butter, chickpeas.
Dal + rice together cover each other's gaps — a complete protein.

blocks.x: What protein does

items.x: Build & repair; leucine triggers synthesis.
Every chemical reaction in the body needs them.
Insulin, growth hormone — all proteins.
Collagen (skin, tendons, bone), keratin (hair).
Every antibody is a protein.
Haemoglobin carries O₂; serotonin & dopamine come from amino acids.

blocks.x: If you run short

steps: Short term: poor recovery, fatigue, slow healing, hair loss, brain fog
Medium term: muscle wasting, fluid retention, hormonal issues, skin loses elasticity
Long term: organ damage, collapsed immunity, kwashiorkor

blocks.x: Master rule: get all 9 essential amino acids daily, prioritise complete sources, and aim for ~2 g per kg bodyweight if you train — eggs and whey first.

topics.name: Fats
topics.blurb: Not the enemy — the type is what matters.

blocks.x: A fat is a glycerol backbone with three fatty-acid chains. Whether it's solid or liquid — and whether it helps or harms — comes down to its chemical shape. There are three broad kinds, and they are not equal.

blocks.cap: The fat family — from best to avoid.

args.title: Prioritise mono & omega-3; moderate saturated; cut omega-6 excess; eliminate trans

blocks.x: The critical number is the Omega-6 : Omega-3 ratio. Ideal is ~4:1; a modern diet runs 20:1 or worse — which is why cutting seed oils and adding fish/flax matters more than total fat.

topics.name: Sugar & Insulin
topics.blurb: The spike, the crash, and the spiral.

blocks.x: Sugar is the fastest carb of all — and its danger is behavioural as much as metabolic. Each hit sets up a short loop that, repeated for years, hardens into insulin resistance.
The meal-to-meal loop

steps: Blood glucose spikes fast → dopamine hit (feels rewarding)
Large insulin response to clear it
Energy crash → brain screams for quick energy

blocks.x: The years-long spiral

As receptors go numb, glucose can't get into cells efficiently — so cells are starved (chronic fatigue) while blood sugar stays high and gets stored as visceral fat, which worsens the resistance further. That's the road to Type 2 diabetes.
The fix isn't heroics — it's blunting the spikes: whole carbs over refined, protein/fat/fibre alongside, and movement after meals to pull glucose into muscle.

tabs.name: Micronutrients
tabs.intro: Vitamins and minerals — tiny amounts, huge leverage, and they interact.

blocks.x: Vitamins split by where they dissolve. Fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) need dietary fat to absorb, are stored in liver and fat, and can accumulate — so you don't need them daily. Water-soluble (B-complex, C) aren't stored, flush out in urine, and are needed regularly (overdose is almost impossible).
Fat-soluble — A · D · E · K

fn: Directs calcium INTO bones, OUT of arteries

blocks.x: Water-soluble — B-complex · C

src: B12: eggs, meat, dairy (supplement if vegan)

blocks.x: For most people on a mixed diet the gaps are: Vitamin D (supplement with food), and B12 (must supplement if plant-based). Get vitamin C from food and A from colourful vegetables daily.

Minerals are inorganic elements. Macrominerals you need in gram amounts — they run structure and fluid balance. Trace minerals you need in milligrams — but they act as catalysts, so a tiny shortfall causes outsized problems.
Macrominerals

fn: 300+ reactions — more than any other mineral

def: Rare; excess is the real issue → high BP

fn: Heart rhythm, counters sodium → lowers BP

def: Extremely rare — bone pain, low energy

blocks.x: Trace minerals

topics.name: Synergies & Antagonisms
topics.blurb: Which nutrients help, block, or activate each other.

blocks.x: Micronutrients don't act alone. Some amplify each other, some compete for the same absorption pathway, and some form chains where one activates the next. Getting the pairings right often matters more than the dose.
Synergistic pairs — take together

items.x: The most critical pairing. D3 absorbs calcium; K2 sends it to bones, not arteries. Always together.
Magnesium activates D3 — without it, a D3 supplement barely works.
Vitamin C converts plant (non-heme) iron into an absorbable form. Lemon on dal.
C regenerates E after it neutralises a free radical — an antioxidant relay.
The bone team. Miss one and the whole chain underperforms.
Iodine makes T4; selenium converts it to active T3. Both needed for thyroid.

blocks.x: Antagonistic pairs — separate them

items.x: Compete for absorption — don't take an iron and a calcium supplement together.
Share a pathway; large doses of one blunt the other.
Compete for the same enzymes — excess omega-6 crowds omega-3 out.

blocks.x: Dependent chains — one activates the next

blocks.cap: Chains where each step enables the next.

tabs.name: Body Systems
tabs.intro: Where nutrition becomes physiology — cholesterol and the stress response.

topics.name: Cholesterol & Lipids
topics.blurb: A building material, not a poison.

blocks.x: Cholesterol is not a disease — it's a fat-like molecule your body depends on. Your liver makes ~80% of it; your brain is ~25% cholesterol; and testosterone, estrogen, cortisol and vitamin D are all built from it. The problem is never that it exists — it's the transport going wrong.
Six essential jobs
LDL and HDL are trucks, not cargo

Cholesterol can't travel in water-based blood alone, so it rides inside lipoproteins. LDL and HDL are the vehicles — not the cholesterol itself. 'LDL cholesterol' on a blood test means the cholesterol being carried inside LDL particles.

Trouble starts when there's too much LDL and it gets oxidised by free radicals — it deposits in artery walls, forming plaque (atherosclerosis). HDL is the cleanup crew pulling excess cholesterol back to the liver.
The reframe: the fix is to improve transport — more HDL, less oxidised LDL — not to crush cholesterol itself. Triglycerides, notably, rise from excess sugar and refined carbs, not dietary fat.

Acute stress is a brilliant survival system: adrenaline and cortisol prepare you to fight or flee — heart rate up, glucose dumped into blood, digestion and immunity paused. The design flaw is that it's meant to be resolved by physical action. When the threat is chronic and psychological, the hormones just circulate with nowhere to go.

blocks.cap: One trigger, one hormone, six simultaneous harms.

blocks.x: Sustained cortisol elevates blood sugar (→ insulin resistance), constricts vessels and retains sodium (→ high blood pressure), breaks down muscle (→ lower testosterone and metabolic rate), stores visceral fat (→ inflammation, worse cholesterol), suppresses immunity, and disrupts sleep — which loops back and worsens everything.

This is why exercise is the most effective stress relief — not as motivation, but as mechanism. Physical activity completes the fight-or-flight cycle: it burns off the adrenaline and cortisol-driven glucose, releases endorphins, and raises testosterone to directly counter cortisol.

tabs.name: Medical Terms
tabs.intro: Common conditions decoded — what they are, why they happen, and the nutrient links.

blocks.x: Most everyday medical terms fall into a few families. Knowing which family something belongs to immediately tells you how it works and why it happens.

items.x: Cholesterol, blood pressure, thyroid — numbers, usually silent until damage is done.
Acidity, constipation, diarrhoea, kidney & gall stones.
Arthritis, asthma, sinusitis, migraine.
Cold and other viral illness — mostly your immune response, not the virus itself.
Migraine, vertigo — and nausea, which is a symptom, not a disease.

items.what: The force of blood against artery walls — systolic (heart contracts) over diastolic (heart relaxes). Normal ~120/80; hypertension is consistently above 130/80.
why: Arteries narrow (plaque) or stiffen (age, trans fats, K2 deficiency)
Excess sodium retains water → more blood volume
Stress and stimulants make the heart pump harder
Kidneys are the master regulator via fluid balance

items.what: The thyroid sets whole-body metabolic speed via hormones T4 and T3. Low output (hypothyroidism) slows every cell.
why: Iodine or selenium deficiency
Autoimmune (Hashimoto's)
sym: Brain fog, dry skin, hair loss

items.what: Stomach acid (HCl) splashes up past the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) into the unprotected esophagus — the burning called heartburn.
why: Overeating or lying down after meals
Obesity, spicy/fatty/acidic foods
Coffee, alcohol, stress; H. pylori
sym: Regurgitation, chronic cough, worse lying down
fix: Smaller meals, don't lie down after eating; treat — don't just mask (chronic reflux → Barrett's esophagus)

items.what: Transit is too slow, so the colon absorbs too much water and stool turns hard and dry (fewer than 3 movements/week).
why: Too little fibre or water
Sedentary living, low magnesium
Hypothyroidism, stress, certain meds
fix: The fibre–water–magnesium triangle solves most cases

items.what: The opposite — transit too fast, or the gut actively secreting water to flush an irritant. Three+ loose stools a day.
why: Viral (rota/noro) or bacterial infection
Food intolerance, IBS, antibiotics
fix: Oral rehydration — water WITH sodium and potassium, not water alone

items.what: Hard mineral crystals that form when urine is too concentrated (80% are calcium-oxalate).
why: Dehydration — urine too concentrated
High sodium, high-oxalate foods, vitamin D excess without K2
sym: Severe cramping flank pain radiating to groin
fix: Drink 2.5–3 L water daily — the single best prevention

items.what: Hard deposits in the gallbladder, usually when bile holds too much cholesterol relative to bile salts (80% are cholesterol stones).
why: High cholesterol in bile, obesity
Rapid weight loss; estrogen; even very low-fat diets (bile stagnates)
sym: Sudden upper-right pain after fatty meals
Radiates to right shoulder; jaundice if duct blocked
fix: Steady weight, regular meals with some fat so the gallbladder empties

items.what: Inflammation of the sinuses; drainage blocks, mucus stagnates and can turn infected.
why: Follows a cold or allergy
Deviated septum, dry air, low immunity
fix: Acute (<4 wks) usually post-cold; chronic (>12 wks) often allergic/structural

items.what: Chronically hypersensitive airways that narrow (bronchospasm), swell and fill with mucus in response to triggers.
why: Allergens, cold air, exercise, infection, smoke
Genetic + early-environment (hygiene hypothesis)
fix: Vitamin D and omega-3 lower severity; magnesium relaxes airways (IV Mg used in severe attacks)

items.what: Joint inflammation — over 100 conditions, but two dominate: osteoarthritis (mechanical cartilage wear) and rheumatoid (autoimmune attack on the joint lining).
why: OA: age, obesity, overuse, injury
RA: genetic predisposition + immune trigger
fix: Omega-3 and vitamin D reduce inflammation; collagen (vitamin-C dependent) and Ca+K2 support joints

items.what: A neurological condition — a wave of electrical activity (cortical spreading depression) triggers inflammation and vessel changes, not just a bad headache.
why: Hormonal shifts, sleep disruption, dehydration, stress
Trigger foods; magnesium deficiency is a top link
sym: Phases: prodrome → aura → throbbing one-sided headache → postdrome
fix: Magnesium, B2 (riboflavin) and CoQ10 all address the mitochondrial/vessel mechanisms

items.what: A viral infection of the nose and throat — 200+ viruses, mostly rhinovirus. The symptoms are your immune response expelling the virus, not the virus itself.
why: Low vitamin D, zinc or vitamin C
Poor sleep, high stress (cortisol suppresses immunity)
fix: No cure — resolves in 7–10 days; vitamin C, zinc, rest, hydration support the response

items.what: A virus is genetic material in a protein shell — not truly alive. It can't reproduce alone; it hijacks your cells' machinery.
why: Enters, injects genes, forces cells to copy it
Spreads until the immune system mounts a response
sym: Depends on the virus (cold, flu, COVID, etc.)
Symptoms are mostly the immune response
fix: Antibiotics don't work — they target bacterial structures viruses lack. Immunity/vaccines build memory B cells

items.what: A symptom, not a disease — the urge to vomit, generated by the brainstem's vomiting centre. Vomiting is a protective reflex to expel a suspected toxin.
why: Gut distress signals via the vagus nerve
Vestibular mismatch (motion sickness), migraine, chemicals, anxiety
fix: Treat the cause; address the underlying trigger

items.what: A false sensation of spinning, distinct from dizziness. Balance comes from inner ear + eyes + joint sensors; when they conflict, you get vertigo.
why: BPPV — dislodged inner-ear calcium crystals (most common)
Vestibular neuritis, Meniere's, migraine-associated
fix: BPPV responds excellently to the Epley maneuver — often 1–2 sessions
